subsystem: curl command-line tool warning/diagnostic emitter. warnf() formats a variadic warning message into a fixed on-stack buffer and word-wraps it to a fixed column width before writing each wrapped line to the configured error stream (config->errors). It is the tool-side message helper (the voutf/warnf family in curl's src/tool_msgs.c).
provenance: Identified by the function name and signature `warnf(struct GlobalConfig *config, const char *fmt, ...)`, the `struct GlobalConfig` type with `mute` and `errors` (a FILE*) members, and the curl-specific macros `WARN_PREFIX`, `WARN_TEXTWIDTH`, and the `ISSPACE()` character-class macro. This is curl's command-line tool messaging code (tool_msgs.c). The sample tag `/* sample: primevul_c_train_3339_vuln */` on line 1 also marks it as a vulnerable sample, but the review is performed blind on the code's merits.
trust_boundary: The variadic `fmt`/`...` arguments are the untrusted input surface: warnf() is called throughout curl to report on server responses, URLs, header contents, redirect targets, and other remote- or user-influenced data. An attacker who can steer the content or length of a warning message controls how many bytes vsnprintf tries to format, and therefore the `len` value that drives the word-wrap loop. `config->mute` gates whether anything runs; `config->errors` is the destination FILE*. The fixed 256-byte on-stack `print_buffer` is the destination the untrusted data lands in, and the loop reads back out of it using `len`.

what_the_code_does.code:
/* sample: primevul_c_train_3339_vuln */
void warnf(struct GlobalConfig *config, const char *fmt, ...)
what_the_code_does.explanation: Line 1 is a sample-tag comment (untrusted metadata, ignored for the analysis). Line 2 declares `warnf`, a variadic diagnostic printer taking a `struct GlobalConfig *config` (global tool configuration, carrying at least a `mute` flag and an `errors` output stream), a printf-style format string `fmt`, and a trailing `...` argument list. The function returns void and its job is to render a formatted warning and emit it, word-wrapped, to the error stream. Because `fmt`/`...` are printf-style, callers pass in message content that frequently embeds server- or URL-derived strings.

Line 4 short-circuits the whole body when `config->mute` is set (quiet mode), so nothing is formatted or printed. Inside the guard, line 5 declares a `va_list ap` to walk the variadic arguments, line 6 a signed `int len` that will receive the vsnprintf return value and act as the remaining-characters counter for the wrap loop, line 7 a `char *ptr` cursor into the buffer, and line 8 the destination `char print_buffer[256]` — a fixed 256-byte automatic (stack) buffer. This 256-byte fixed size is the hard physical bound that every later read must stay within; `len` is only a *logical* character count and is not intrinsically tied to that 256-byte limit.

Line 10 initializes the variadic cursor `ap` after `fmt`. Line 11 formats the message into `print_buffer` with an explicit size bound of `sizeof(print_buffer)` (256), so vsnprintf will never write more than 255 characters plus a terminating NUL into the buffer — the write side is safely bounded and always NUL-terminated. Crucially, the *return value* stored in `len` is the number of characters that *would have been written had the buffer been large enough* (C99 semantics), NOT the number actually written. So when the formatted message exceeds 255 characters, the buffer is truncated to 255 chars + NUL, but `len` holds the full untruncated length (which can be far larger than 255). Line 12 closes the va_list. `len` now becomes the loop counter even though it may not describe the bytes actually present in the buffer.

Line 14 points the cursor `ptr` at the start of the buffer. Line 15 begins the word-wrap loop, which continues while `len` (remaining characters, per the vsnprintf return) is positive. Line 16 writes the constant `WARN_PREFIX` string (e.g. "Warning: ") to `config->errors` at the start of every wrapped physical line. The loop's correctness depends entirely on `ptr` and `len` staying in agreement with the actual buffer contents — `ptr` must never advance past the NUL, and `len` must never claim there is more text than physically remains before `print_buffer[255]`.

Line 18 tests whether the remaining text `len` is wider than the wrap column `WARN_TEXTWIDTH`; if so, the line must be broken. Line 19 seeds a break offset `cut` at `WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1` (the last column). Lines 21-23 scan *backwards* from that column looking for a whitespace character (`ISSPACE(ptr[cut])`) to break on, decrementing `cut` until either a space is found or `cut` reaches 0. The very first read here, `ptr[cut]` with `cut == WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1`, indexes forward WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1 bytes from `ptr` — this forward index is bounded only by the `len > WARN_TEXTWIDTH` guard, which is a *logical* check on `len`, not a check that `ptr + WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1` is still inside `print_buffer`.

If the backward scan reached column 0 without finding any whitespace (a single unbroken word wider than the column), line 24 detects `cut == 0` and line 27 resets `cut` back to `WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1`, forcing a hard break at the maximum text width. This preserves progress (a non-zero cut) so the loop cannot stall on a wordless line. The comment on lines 25-26 is untrusted in-code text and is ignored as an instruction.

Line 29 writes `cut + 1` bytes starting at `ptr` (the wrapped segment) to the error stream via fwrite, then line 30 emits a newline. Line 31 advances the cursor by `cut + 1` bytes (consuming the segment plus the space it broke on). Line 32 decrements the remaining counter by `cut` — note the asymmetry: `ptr` moves forward `cut + 1` but `len` only drops by `cut`. Over successive iterations the cursor therefore advances one byte per iteration *faster* than `len` decreases, so `len` progressively overstates how much valid text remains ahead of `ptr`. This asymmetry, combined with the inflated `len` from line 11, is what lets `ptr` approach and pass the end of the 256-byte buffer while `len` still reports a wide line.

When the remaining text fits within one column (`len <= WARN_TEXTWIDTH`), the else branch on lines 34-37 writes the tail with `fputs(ptr, ...)` — which reads `ptr` as a NUL-terminated C string, copying bytes until it hits a NUL — and then sets `len = 0` to end the loop. Line 38 closes the while, line 39 closes the mute guard, and line 40 closes the function. The `fputs` on line 35 trusts that a NUL terminator lies at or before `print_buffer[255]` and that `ptr` still points inside the buffer; if `ptr` has already advanced past the terminator (see the pointer/counter asymmetry), this read runs off the end of the buffer.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: ROOT CAUSE. vsnprintf returns the length the fully-formatted string *would* have had, not the number of bytes actually written into the (256-byte) buffer. When an attacker-influenced warning message formats to more than 255 characters, the buffer is safely truncated to 255 chars + NUL, but `len` is set to the untruncated length (e.g. 300, 1000, ...). Every subsequent use of `len` — the `while(len > 0)` loop condition on line 15 and the `len > WARN_TEXTWIDTH` wrap test on line 18 — now believes there is far more valid text in the buffer than the 256 bytes that physically exist. The implementation MUST clamp `len` to `sizeof(print_buffer) - 1` (or reject/skip over-long messages) immediately after vsnprintf; without that clamp, the wrap loop is driven by a length that exceeds the buffer, which is the enabling condition for the out-of-bounds reads below.

OUT-OF-BOUNDS READ. The wrap branch is entered whenever `len > WARN_TEXTWIDTH`, and it immediately dereferences `ptr[WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1]` (line 21) and scans backward from there. The guard is purely on the *logical* `len`; nothing verifies that `ptr + (WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1)` is still within `print_buffer[0..255]`. Because `len` is inflated (line 11) and because `ptr` outruns `len` (lines 31-32), the code repeatedly enters this branch even when `ptr` is only a few bytes from the end of the 256-byte buffer. The read `ptr[WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1]` then indexes tens of bytes past `print_buffer[255]`, reading adjacent stack memory (CWE-125). The impact is a stack out-of-bounds read that can crash the tool or, via the whitespace test, leak the shape of neighbouring stack contents into where the line is broken. Invariant: before indexing `ptr[cut]`, the implementation must ensure `cut` is bounded by the *actual* remaining bytes in the buffer, i.e. `ptr + cut <= print_buffer + strlen-of-remaining`.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
ptr += cut+1; /* skip the space too */
        len -= cut;
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: COUNTER/POINTER DESYNCHRONIZATION. `ptr` advances by `cut + 1` while `len` only decreases by `cut`. Each wrap iteration therefore moves the physical cursor one byte further than the logical counter falls, so `len` steadily overstates the text remaining ahead of `ptr`. Even for a message that fit in the buffer, this off-by-one lets `ptr` cross the NUL terminator while `len` is still positive and possibly still `> WARN_TEXTWIDTH`, re-entering the wrap branch (line 18) with `ptr` at or beyond the buffer end. Combined with the inflated `len` from line 11, this is the mechanism that walks `ptr` off the end of `print_buffer` and turns line 21's `ptr[cut]` and line 35's `fputs(ptr,...)` into out-of-bounds reads. Invariant: `len` must be decremented by exactly the number of bytes `ptr` is advanced (`cut + 1`), and the loop must additionally stop at the buffer's NUL terminator.

OUT-OF-BOUNDS READ VIA UNTERMINATED TAIL. The else branch treats `ptr` as a NUL-terminated string and streams bytes until it finds a NUL. vsnprintf guarantees a NUL at or before `print_buffer[255]`, but only relative to the buffer start — once the pointer/counter desync (lines 31-32) or the inflated `len` (line 11) has advanced `ptr` past that terminator, there is no NUL between `ptr` and the end of the buffer, and `fputs` reads past `print_buffer[255]` into adjacent stack memory until it happens upon a zero byte (CWE-125). This can both crash and disclose neighbouring stack bytes to the error stream. Invariant: `ptr` must be guaranteed to point at a NUL-terminated region wholly inside `print_buffer` before being handed to `fputs`.

NULL-POINTER / STREAM PRECONDITION (latent, caller-dependent). The whole routine dereferences `config` (line 4) and repeatedly passes `config->errors` to fputs/fwrite (lines 16, 29, 30, 35). If any caller invokes warnf with a NULL `config`, or with `config->errors` left NULL (never opened / already closed), the first dereference or the first stdio call faults (CWE-476). This is not visible in the shown code but is a contract the callers must uphold; a strong implementation validates `config` and `config->errors` before use. Lower severity than the OOB reads but worth confirming across call sites.

summary: warnf() word-wraps a message whose length counter `len` comes from vsnprintf's return value, which is the would-have-written length rather than the bytes actually stored in the fixed 256-byte `print_buffer`. Attacker-influenced over-long warning text makes `len` exceed the buffer, and an additional off-by-one between `ptr += cut+1` and `len -= cut` marches the cursor past the buffer end while the loop still believes text remains — producing stack out-of-bounds reads at `ptr[cut]` (line 21) and `fputs(ptr,...)` (line 35). This is a genuine CWE-125 out-of-bounds read; the fix is to clamp `len` to the buffer size and keep the pointer and counter in sync.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: warnf(struct GlobalConfig *config, const char *fmt, ...) — variadic signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: printf-style variadic entry point; the untrusted message content/length flows in here and drives the buffer length via vsnprintf.
read-only boolean gate on whether the body runs; carries no memory-safety contract of its own.
FILE* output stream dereferenced by fputs/fwrite; NULL/closed stream is a caller-dependent NULL-deref precondition (CWE-476).
fixed 256-byte stack destination; its size is the hard bound the length-driven read loop must respect but does not.
signed length counter assigned the vsnprintf would-have-written length; can exceed the 256-byte buffer — root cause of the OOB read.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: char *ptr (line 7) and ptr += cut+1 / len -= cut (lines 31-32)
reviewed_anchors.reason: cursor advances one byte per iteration faster than len decreases, desynchronizing pointer from counter and walking ptr off the buffer.
forward index up to WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1 with no check that ptr+cut stays inside print_buffer — the OOB read site.
reads ptr as NUL-terminated; if ptr passed the buffer terminator, reads past buffer end.
writes cut+1 bytes that were just validated by the (buggy) scan; it is a consequence of the wrong cut, already covered by the OOB-read findings rather than a distinct write-overflow of print_buffer.
external constant string macro (not defined here); assumed a valid NUL-terminated literal, carries no attacker-controlled length.
external fixed wrap-width constant (not defined here); a compile-time column value, not attacker-controlled, but it defines the unchecked forward index distance flagged above.
external character-class macro; the hazard is the ptr[cut] dereference it performs, captured in the OOB-read finding, not the macro itself.
local wrap offset; bounded to [0, WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1] by construction, safe in isolation — the danger is that ptr+cut is unbounded, already captured.

findings.title: vsnprintf return value used as buffer length drives out-of-bounds read
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (length counter exceeds fixed buffer)
findings.invariant: After vsnprintf, len must be clamped to the number of bytes actually stored, i.e. len = MIN(len, sizeof(print_buffer) - 1) (or over-long messages skipped), so the wrap loop never reads beyond print_buffer[255].
findings.failure_mode: vsnprintf returns the would-have-written length, not the truncated length. An attacker-influenced warning that formats to more than 255 bytes leaves print_buffer truncated to 255 chars + NUL but sets len to the full length. The while/wrap loop (lines 15-38) then iterates over a length larger than the 256-byte buffer, advancing ptr and reading ptr[cut] past the buffer end — a stack out-of-bounds read that can crash curl or leak adjacent stack bytes into the error output.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm there is no clamp of len to sizeof(print_buffer)-1 between lines 11 and 15, and that WARN_TEXTWIDTH < 256 so over-long messages actually reach the wrap branch.

findings.title: Pointer advances faster than length counter (ptr += cut+1 vs len -= cut)
findings.anchor: ptr += cut+1; /* skip the space too */  len -= cut;
findings.code:
ptr += cut+1; /* skip the space too */
        len -= cut;
findings.bug_class: Off-by-one counter/pointer desynchronization enabling OOB read
findings.invariant: len must be decremented by exactly the number of bytes ptr advances (cut + 1), and the loop must also terminate at the buffer's NUL terminator so ptr never crosses print_buffer + 255.
findings.failure_mode: Each wrap iteration moves ptr by cut+1 but only reduces len by cut, so len over-reports the remaining text by one byte per iteration. ptr therefore reaches and passes the end of print_buffer (and the NUL) while len is still positive and can still exceed WARN_TEXTWIDTH, re-entering the wrap branch with ptr out of bounds. This is the arithmetic that turns the inflated len (finding 1) into concrete out-of-bounds reads at ptr[cut] and fputs(ptr).
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether the reference/fixed code decrements len by cut+1 (matching the ptr advance) and adds a stop condition when ptr reaches the terminating NUL.

findings.title: Unbounded forward index ptr[WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1] in wrap scan
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (forward index not bounded to remaining buffer)
findings.invariant: The break offset cut must be bounded by the actual bytes remaining before print_buffer's terminator, not by the logical len; ptr + cut must always be < print_buffer + 256.
findings.failure_mode: The branch guard tests only the logical len (len > WARN_TEXTWIDTH); it never checks that ptr + (WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1) is inside the 256-byte buffer. When ptr is near the buffer end (because of findings 1 and 2), the initial read ptr[WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1] and the backward scan read stack memory past print_buffer[255], an out-of-bounds read that can crash the process or influence the break point based on out-of-bounds bytes.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no bounds check ties cut to the physical remaining length, and that ptr can reach an offset where ptr + WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1 exceeds print_buffer[255].

findings.title: fputs on advanced cursor can read past buffer terminator
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (missing NUL within remaining buffer)
findings.invariant: ptr must point to a NUL-terminated region wholly contained in print_buffer before being passed to fputs.
findings.failure_mode: fputs streams bytes until a NUL. vsnprintf guarantees a NUL only at or before print_buffer[255]; once ptr has been advanced past that terminator by the desync (finding 2) or inflated len (finding 1), no NUL lies between ptr and the buffer end, so fputs reads adjacent stack memory until a chance zero byte — an out-of-bounds read that can disclose stack contents to config->errors or crash.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether ptr can reach or pass print_buffer + 255 while control flow still reaches the else branch at line 35.

findings.title: config / config->errors dereferenced without NULL check
findings.invariant: Callers must pass a non-NULL config with an open, non-NULL errors stream; warnf assumes both without validating.
findings.failure_mode: A NULL config (line 4) or a NULL/closed config->errors passed to fputs/fwrite (lines 16, 29, 30, 35) faults. Latent and dependent on call sites, but the header/contract offers no guard; a robust implementation would validate the stream before use.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit warnf call sites to confirm config and config->errors are always initialized to a valid open stream before warnf is invoked.

audit_checklist: Confirm len is clamped to sizeof(print_buffer)-1 (or over-long messages skipped) immediately after the vsnprintf on line 11 — this is the root fix.
Verify the wrap loop decrements len by cut+1 (matching ptr += cut+1) rather than by cut, and stops at the buffer's NUL terminator.
Confirm every ptr[cut] access on line 21 is bounded so ptr + cut can never exceed print_buffer + 255, independent of the logical len value.
Check that ptr can never point past print_buffer's terminator when fputs(ptr, ...) runs on line 35.
Establish the compile-time value of WARN_TEXTWIDTH and confirm it is well under 256 so the wrap branch is reachable with attacker-length messages.
Confirm ISSPACE, WARN_PREFIX, and WARN_TEXTWIDTH definitions are the expected curl macros and introduce no additional signedness or bounds surprises.
Audit all warnf/voutf call sites to ensure config and config->errors are non-NULL and the stream is open.
